# US Stock Watcher Next-Session Forecast — Tuesday 7/21

> **[Tuesday 7/21 session outlook] bullish lean — Moderate / selective risk-on · composite +17 · confidence 39%**

> **🔮 Next-session forecast** · The next U.S. regular session is Tuesday 7/21 (opens 9:30 AM ET / 22:30 KST 7/21).

_Data as of: 2026-07-21T02:56:53 · data quality: fresh_

## Executive summary

With the U.S. market currently closed, this brief looks ahead to the next regular session — Tuesday, July 21, opening at 9:30 AM ET (22:30 KST on 7/21). Heading into that open, the overall market-state score stands at +17 out of 100, placing conditions in a "Moderate / selective risk-on" regime, though confidence in that read is modest at 39%, based on four measured components. Several inputs remain unmeasured for this cycle — Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning have all been reweighted out of the current composite — so the signal should be treated as directional rather than definitive.

## Market diagnosis

**Indices.** Going into Tuesday's session, the major benchmarks closed as follows: the S&P 500 at 7,443.28 (-0.19% on the day), the Nasdaq-100 at 28,604.23 (+0.04%), the Dow at 51,839.26 (-0.59%), and the Russell 2000 at 2,942.43 (-0.67%). The mixed picture — large-cap tech holding roughly flat while the Dow and small caps lagged — sets a somewhat uneven baseline for the next open.

**Market drivers.** Four forces are shaping the setup: volatility is acting as a headwind, while index trend and the yield curve are both supportive. The U.S. dollar, meanwhile, is functioning as a headwind to risk appetite.

**Breadth & concentration.** The composite market-state score of +17/100 continues to point to a "Moderate / selective risk-on" posture, with confidence held at 39% on four measured components. As noted above, Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning are currently unmeasured and have been reweighted out of the calculation, which tempers conviction in the signal.

**Sector & rotation.** Leadership is concentrated in Energy, Health Care, and Financials, while Industrials, Materials, and Technology are lagging — a rotation pattern worth watching as the next session opens, particularly given the flat Nasdaq-100 print against Technology's laggard status.

**Rates, dollar & commodities.** The 10-year Treasury yield sits at 4.55%. The Dollar Index (DXY) is at 100.98, down -0.01% on the day. In commodities, WTI crude is at 82.48 (-0.01%), while gold has advanced to 4,040.00, up +0.68% — a notable bid for the metal even as the dollar holds steady.

## Outlook

**Short term.** Framed for Tuesday's open (9:30 AM ET / 22:30 KST), the near-term bias is constructive, consistent with the "Moderate / selective risk-on" market state and its 39% confidence reading. That said, this lean is conditional: should the overall composite score cross back below zero, the outlook would flip.

**Medium term.** The medium-term view carries the same constructive bias, anchored to the "Moderate / selective risk-on" state and 39% confidence level, with the same caveat — a move of the overall score below zero would invalidate the current lean.

**Medium-to-long term.** Extending further out, the medium-to-long-term stance remains constructive on the same basis — the "Moderate / selective risk-on" read at 39% confidence — and would likewise reverse if the overall score turns negative.

Heading into the session, headlines include: "Dollar near one-week high as markets grapple with Gulf tensions" (1450 AM 99.7 FM WHTC); "Jamie Dimon Won't Buy S&P 500 or Bonds. Here Are the Warnings Investors Are Missing" (Yahoo Finance); "Nasdaq, S&P 500 Futures Rise As Chip Rally Counters Iran Jitters Ahead Of Big Tech Earnings: Why IREN, ACHR, TSLA, BA Stocks Are Drawing Focus" (TradingView); "Korea's Economic Growth Driven by AI Demand, Semiconductor Exports" (GuruFocus); a report that Morgan Stanley, a global investment bank, predicted a supply shortage in the memory semiconductor space (매일경제); and "Stock Market News, July 20, 2026: Chip Stocks Pare Gains as S&P 500 Finishes Lower" (WSJ).
